name: Tour of Valderejo and Montes Obarenes Natural Parks
name_original: Cueva de los Moros y Torre de los Verona  — circular por la Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche
description: This moderate 58.3-mile road cycling route through Valderejo and Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Parks visits Cueva de los Moros and To
region_intro: Road cycling around Trespaderne, located in the province of Burgos, Spain, offers diverse landscapes for cyclists. The region is characterized by its varied terrain, including areas within the Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park, river valleys carved by the Ebro, Nela, and Jerea rivers, and quiet rural roads. Cyclists can expect significant elevation gains and a mix of farmlands, peaceful villages, and hills. Best road cycling routes around Trespaderne The most popular road cycling route is Tour of Valderejo and Montes Obarenes Natural Parks, a 58.3 miles (93.8 km) trail that takes 4 hours 7 minutes to complete. This moderate route features scenic views through two natural parks. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Oña Historic Town Center – Portillo de Busto loop from Cantabrana, a moderate 42.0 miles (67.6 km) path. This route offers a journey through varied terrain, connecting historic towns and rural landscapes. Local road cyclists also love the Frías Bridge – Oña Historic Town Center loop from Frías, a 39.7 miles (63.9 km) trail leading through historical sites and river valleys, often completed in about 3 hours. Road cycling around Trespaderne is defined by its natural parks, quiet rural roads, and diverse scenery ranging from farmlands to hills. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Trespaderne are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Trespaderne's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Trespaderne?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes around Trespaderne, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. These routes cater to various preferences, from moderate to challenging.

Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty can I expect on road cycling routes near Trespaderne?
A: The region offers varied terrain, from farmlands and peaceful villages to hills of saline marl and ancient volcanic formations. You can expect routes with significant elevation gains. Out of the 24 available routes, 14 are considered moderate, and 9 are difficult, with one easy option for a more relaxed ride.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Trespaderne?
A: While many routes feature significant elevation, there is at least one easy road cycling route available. Additionally, the Santander-Mediterranean Greenway offers a traffic-free experience, though its surface quality can vary. For specific family-friendly options, look for routes with lower elevation gain and shorter distances.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see while road cycling around Trespaderne?
A: Many routes pass through the beautiful &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6318262" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montes Obarenes-San Zadornil Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, offering geological and geomorphological interest. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3079476" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Purón River Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, or scenic waterfalls like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1871481" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñón Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. The region is also characterized by the Ebro, Nela, and Jerea river valleys.

Q: Are there any historical sites or interesting towns to visit along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, several routes connect to historical sites and charming towns. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20160360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oña Historic Town Center – Portillo de Busto loop from Cantabrana&lt;/a&gt; takes you through historic towns. You can also visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6365773" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oña Historic Center&lt;/a&gt;, the medieval town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6302190" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Frías&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4987028" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Saint Mary of La Hoz&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Trespaderne?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 8 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse scenery, ranging from natural parks to quiet rural roads, and the challenging yet rewarding elevation gains.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, there are several longer routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31902567" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Presa de Sobrón Road – Frías Bridge loop from Cantabrana&lt;/a&gt; is over 100 kilometers (62 miles) long, offering a significant challenge for experienced cyclists.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Trespaderne?
A: Many of the road cycling routes around Trespaderne are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1060483565/tour-of-valderejo-and-montes-obarenes-natural-parks" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tour of Valderejo and Montes Obarenes Natural Parks&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24569178" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Frías Bridge – Oña Historic Town Center loop from Frías&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Trespaderne?
A: The region's diverse landscapes are enjoyable across seasons. However, for optimal road cycling conditions, spring and autumn generally offer milder temperatures and pleasant scenery. Summer can be warm, while winter might bring cooler temperatures, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any specific starting points or parking facilities for road cyclists in Trespaderne?
A: While specific parking facilities aren't detailed, many routes are designed as loops, implying convenient starting points within or near towns like Trespaderne or Cantabrana. Look for public parking areas in these villages when planning your ride.

Q: Are there any less crowded road cycling routes in the area?
A: Beyond dedicated cycle paths like sections of the Santander-Mediterranean Greenway, the region is known for its quiet rural roads. Exploring routes that venture deeper into the natural parks or less populated areas will likely offer a more tranquil cycling experience away from traffic.

Q: What are some of the most challenging road cycling routes near Trespaderne?
A: For those seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20159704" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oña Historic Town Center – MonasterioDeSanSalvador loop from Cantabrana&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 1,000 meters of elevation gain, are considered difficult. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31902567" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Presa de Sobrón Road – Frías Bridge loop from Cantabrana&lt;/a&gt; is another demanding option, covering a long distance with significant climbing.
tips: The Torre de los Varona is located in the town of Villanañe, in the Spanish province of Álava. It is a fortress that has belonged uninterruptedly since the 15th century to the Los Varona family, who were of vital importance both in the wars between Castile and Aragon and in the Carlist Wars.
Beautiful road that is located next to the Sobrón Dam and that follows the waters of the Ebro river itself in these areas that form the border between the two provinces.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular road cycling route is approximately 93.8 kilometers long and typically takes around 4 hours and 8 minutes to complete. This duration is for the cycling itself and does not include stops for rest or sightseeing.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is generally not recommended for beginners. The Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche region is known for its hilly landscape and significant elevation changes, requiring good to very good fitness. While rated 'moderate', the terrain can be challenging for those new to road cycling in mountainous areas.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling in the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche?
faqs.answer: The terrain is characterized by a hilly landscape with gently rounded mountains and wide valleys. You'll primarily be on an extensive network of public roads linking towns and villages. Expect significant climbs and descents, as the area is quite rugged, even on paved surfaces. The scenery is often green and leafy, with wooded areas of holm oak, cork oak, and chestnut forests.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along this route?
faqs.answer: This route takes you past several notable highlights. You'll encounter the intriguing Cueva de los Moros (Cave of the Moors) and the historic Torre de los Verona (Verona Tower). Additionally, the route passes by the scenic Presa de Sobrón Road, offering picturesque views.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to road cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Aracena benefits from Atlantic weather systems, making it greener than other parts of Southern Spain. While specific best times aren't detailed, generally, spring (March-May) and autumn (September-November) offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential chill of winter.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is primarily a road cycling route on public roads, dogs are generally permitted, especially if kept on a leash and under control. However, always be mindful of local regulations, especially when passing through villages or near protected natural areas within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need special permits or to pay entrance fees to cycle this route. It traverses public roads within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park, which is freely accessible. However, always respect local rules and private property.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: Since this is a circular route, you can typically start and park in any of the towns or villages along the route within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche. Look for public parking areas in these villages. Specific trailhead parking is not usually designated for road cycling routes of this nature.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with the Asturian Way, Ibilbide historikoa: Araba, and Ruta de los Monteros del Rey. Smaller segments also touch upon Artzaintza naturbidea, Sobrón Alto (R11), and the Camino natural de Ebro - Castilla y León, among others.

faqs.question: What should I pack for road cycling in the Sierra de Aracena?
faqs.answer: Given the route's length and difficulty, pack essentials like sufficient water and snacks, a repair kit for your bike (spare tube, pump, tools), appropriate cycling attire for varying temperatures, sun protection, and a first-aid kit. A fully charged phone and navigation device are also highly recommended due to the extensive network of roads.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenges or insider tips for this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The primary challenge is the consistent elevation gain and loss throughout the route, requiring good stamina. An insider tip for this region is to plan your stops in the 'prettiest white villages' nestled in the valleys. These offer charming architectural sights and opportunities to experience the local gastronomy, particularly the famous acorn-fed Iberian ham, making for excellent mid-ride breaks.
